perturbation.original_text: "Gross Revenues" means the gross revenues received by the Licensee and derived from or in connection with the Licensee Site, from any sources whatsoever, including but not limited to fees for access to and use of the Licensee Site and advertising, sponsorship, marketing and other paid-for placements, andmaintenance, support and other services.
perturbation.changed_text: "Gross Revenues" means the gross revenues received by the Licensee and derived from only the direct fees collected for access to the Licensee Site. Revenues from advertising, sponsorship, marketing, and support services are specifically excluded.
perturbation.explanation: This modification changes the definition of "Gross Revenues" to exclude advertising and marketing revenue. This will impact royalty calculations and potentially lead to disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts 26 U.S.C. § 61, which broadly defines gross income to include all income from whatever source derived, including advertising and services. The contract now attempts to narrowly define it for royalty purposes in a way that is inconsistent with the federal tax code.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 61 - Gross income defined | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 61 - Gross income defined U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) General definition Except as otherwise provided in this subtitle, gross income means all income from whatever source derived, including (but not limited to) the following items: (1) Compensation for services, including fees, commissions, fringe benefits, and similar items; (2) Gross income derived from business; (3) Gains derived from dealings in property; (4) Interest; (5) Rents; (6) Royalties; (7) Dividends; (8) Annuities; (9) Income from life insurance and endowment contracts; (10) Pensions; (11) Income from discharge of indebtedness; (12) Distributive share of partnership gross income; (13) Income in respect of a decedent; and (14) Income from an interest in an estate or trust. (b) Cross references For items specifically included in gross income, see part II (sec. 71 and following). For items specifically excluded from gross income, see part III (sec. 101 and following). perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 26 U.S.C. § 61 by redefining "Gross Revenues" in a way that excludes income sources (advertising, sponsorship, marketing, and support services) that would normally be considered gross income under the federal tax code. The original text includes these sources, while the modified text specifically excludes them for royalty calculation. Both scraped law snippets define gross income broadly as 'all income from whatever source derived.' By excluding advertising and service revenue, the modified contract attempts to circumvent this definition for royalty purposes, which creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The relationship between the parties is that of independent contractors. Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed as creating any agency, partnership, joint venture or other form of joint enterprise, employment or fiduciary relationship between the parties, and neither party shall have authority to contract for or bind the other party in any manner whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: The relationship between the parties is that of independent contractors; however, Licensee shall act as Licensor's agent for the purposes of marketing the Licensed Content, and Licensee shall have the authority to enter into agreements with advertisers on Licensor's behalf.
perturbation.explanation: This modification creates a contradiction by stating the parties are independent contractors while simultaneously granting agency authority to the Licensee, which is inconsistent with the independent contractor status.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Third) of Agency § 1.01 defines agency as the fiduciary relationship that arises when one person (a “principal”) manifests assent to another person (an “agent”) that the agent shall act on the principal’s behalf and subject to the principal’s control, and the agent manifests assent or otherwise consents so to act. The original text correctly identifies them as independent contractors where there is no such control. This conflicts with this definition as there is no longer a defined 'fiduciary relationship'.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly establishes an independent contractor relationship, precluding agency. The changed text directly contradicts this by granting the Licensee agency authority to act on the Licensor's behalf and enter into agreements, thus creating a direct conflict. The Law Explanation supports this by defining agency as a fiduciary relationship where an agent acts on behalf of a principal, subject to their control. This is inconsistent with the independent contractor status initially established. The misaligned terminology, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation, arises from using 'independent contractor' while simultaneously granting agency authority, a contradiction that undermines the original agreement's intent. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the combined effect of the changed text, Perturbation Explanation, and Law Explanation points to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal laws of the State of Nevada without giving effect to any choice or conflict of law provision or rule.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Hong Kong, notwithstanding the fact that the Licensee's operations and the content delivery primarily occur within the United States.
perturbation.explanation: This modification changes the governing law to Hong Kong, even though the Licensee is a Nevada corporation operating in the US and the content is delivered there. This conflicts with standard conflict of law principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 states that the law of the state chosen by the parties to govern their contractual rights and duties will be applied if the particular issue is one which the parties could have resolved by an explicit provision in their agreement directed to that issue.  However, the application of the law of the chosen state would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue and which would be the state of the applicable law in the absence of an effective choice of law by the parties. Because the new language specifies Hong Kong, even though the transaction occurs between a Nevada corporation and consumers in North America, the new language is in contradiction.

perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from Nevada to Hong Kong, despite the Licensee's US operations. The LLM's Law Explanation cites the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, stating that the chosen law applies unless it contradicts a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest. Since the operation occurs in the US, applying Hong Kong law likely contradicts this principle. The change introduces misaligned terminology, as Hong Kong law might not align with US legal standards for a Nevada corporation operating primarily in the US, potentially making the contract unenforceable. Although the Scraped Law Snippets are not directly helpful, the combination of the Perturbation Explanation, Law Explanation, and the definition for Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction clearly indicate a legally significant contradiction.
